availability.p:
Published under a
Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 31 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: ¶ So that princes and governors of the republic may weigh such cases more carefully: I will also prove by other arguments that it is not lawful to kill or inflict harm on many, indeed not even on a few innocent persons: so that a few innocent persons may be freed from death: and that a prince or governor doing or permitting otherwise, sins mortally and is bound to restitution. Which is proved first concerning sin, since in such a case it is per se to kill an innocent person. Therefore it is a mortal sin: but here many are killed or afflicted with harm who deserve no such thing, as appears from what has been said. Therefore such a prince sins mortally.

Secondly, because this work is impious and ungrateful in the eyes of God: no differently than the sacrifice of Cain or the vow of Jephthah who sacrificed his own daughter Judges 11. whom
Augustine
affirms to have acted savagely and cruelly. Agamemnon did similarly, who sacrificed his daughter Iphigenia to Diana by vow, whose deed
Tully On Duties book 3
relates and reproves.

Thirdly, such a work is iniquitous and indiscreet which is offered to God lacking the proper circumstances of place, time and things which are not pleasing to God. For God does not rejoice in the sorrow of the innocent and harmless: whom to kill is a greater sin than what the pagans commit by sacrificing the innocent. For they, by probable ignorance (as I will say below), sacrifice thirty or a hundred or a thousand each year: but those, namely the soldiers: waging war for this cause, on one day slaughter ten thousand innocent persons with great loss of their own souls. For since they have been instructed in the doctrine of Christ: they ought not to be ignorant that the innocent should be spared: whence they do not divide rightly, as
Augustine said above concerning Cain . Therefore they are guilty of the most serious crime before God, worthy of eternal damnation.

¶ As for what pertains to restitution, the common opinion of the doctors is: that plunder seized from the innocent who have no guilt of war: must necessarily be restored.
St. Thomas 2a 2ae q. 40. art. 1
gives the reason, namely that war is licit from the fault of those against whom it is waged. But innocent and guiltless men have deserved no such thing. Therefore with regard to them the war was not licit. But what is seized in an unjust war must necessarily be restored.

Concerning this
St. Thomas also treats 2a 2ae. q. 66. art. 8 in the body and in response to the first : and it is proved in c. sicut in 3. de jure eius, and from the doctors there: and speaking specifically of the innocent to whom harm is done not per accidens, as in the example given above:
Raymond teaches in his summa, title on war §. 17. and §. 19 . in these words:
subjects who do not wish to render counsel and aid or favor to their lord in an illicit war: I believe should in no way be despoiled: or punished in any way. For punishment ought to hold its authors: nor should it be extended further than the offense is found in the transgressor
extravagantes de his quae fiunt par. cap. c. quaesiuit . and. 1. q. 4, c. 1.

Ulrich disciple of Albert the Great also teaches in his summa lib. 6. tract. 3. c. 7 . and adds in c. 9.
The third condition, that subjects should not be harmed even if the lord in an unjust war uses their things, violently taken, in support of his war . these are his words.
Hostiensis also teaches in summa title on penance and remission § to whom and how and by whom verse c. what concerning plunder . It is also found in summa confessorum lib. 2. title 5. question. 44.
Astesanus proves the same in summa 1st part lib. 1. title 32. art. 6 . and lib. 2. title 64. art. 7.

This is also proved by
summa Silvestrina war 1. §. 10. 3rd part , in these words:
one having a just war or finding himself in it, if he has despoiled clerics / religious / or converts or pilgrims, although they were with the enemies: is bound to restore, and sins mortally. Such commit robbery if they despoil rustics or travelers or those engaged in agriculture: and animals with which they plow and carry seeds. Indeed subjects of a lord unjustly waging war, not giving counsel, aid or favor directly or indirectly, should not be despoiled or punished in anything: but they are bound to restore lest the penalty exceed the authors. By stronger reason, however, one is bound to restore who harms innocents not subject to the enemy: unless he does this beyond his intention: for instance if, not being able to capture the city, he gives it to sack, or burns it: not otherwise being able to punish many guilty ones . these are his words.

By these words of Silvestri it is proved: that the innocent should not be vexed or harmed: unless when it cannot otherwise be done according to what was said in the preceding chapter: and that so that a few guilty may be punished: the most innocent should not be harmed: and that one acting otherwise sins mortally, and is bound to restitution, and are considered murderers of those innocents. The same is taught by
Brother John of Tabia in his summa which is called Tabiana in the word war §. 8 . And
the most learned father Francisco de Vitoria in the 2nd relection on the Indies .

Therefore, since in those provinces where infidels eat human flesh and sacrifice innocents: there are few who commit these crimes: but innumerable are those who are immune from these crimes, indeed they do not even participate in any way in those things, and since we ought not expose to danger many innocents for the sake of freeing a few, even though innocent: it follows that the church ought not wage such war, nor any other prince or member of the church, since they do this not under the pretext of defending their own kingdoms, which are far removed from the kingdoms of the infidels: but only under the pretext of freeing the innocent: when in truth an infinite multitude of innocents would be destroyed under this pretext.

And so it does not militate against the predetermined according to the second argument, since that whole republic neither is nor should be presumed according to jurisdiction to be an enemy or hostile, but rather innocent and friendly.
